input: Compare naive RAG vs sentence-window retrieval
expected_output: Naive RAG retrieves fixed-size document chunks based on embedding similarity. Sentence-window retrieval indexes individual sentences but expands each hit to a surrounding window of sentences before passing the context to the LLM, yielding more coherent and precise results than naive chunking.
notes: Core domain topic. Local knowledge base (PDFs) has direct coverage.

input: What are the key differences between BM25 and semantic search in RAG systems?
expected_output: BM25 is a sparse keyword-based retrieval algorithm that scores documents by term frequency and inverse document frequency. Semantic search uses dense vector embeddings to capture meaning beyond exact keyword matches. RAG systems often combine both via hybrid search to leverage the complementary strengths of each approach.
notes: Well-defined technical comparison. Covered in the ingested PDFs.

input: What is retrieval-augmented generation and why is it better than vanilla LLM?
expected_output: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) combines a language model with a retrieval system to ground responses in external documents. Unlike a vanilla LLM, RAG can access up-to-date information, cite sources, and reduce hallucinations by anchoring answers to retrieved evidence.
notes: Foundational RAG concept. Directly covered in retrieval-augmented-generation.pdf.

input: How does reranking improve RAG retrieval quality? Give specific algorithms.
expected_output: Reranking applies a cross-encoder or a learned model to re-score initially retrieved candidates, promoting the most relevant documents. Common approaches include Cohere Rerank, Colbert, and BM25 fusion. This two-stage pipeline compensates for the recall-oriented but precision-limited nature of ANN-based first-pass retrieval.
notes: Multi-step topic requiring both local knowledge and web search for specific algorithms.

input: Explain how LLM agents use tool calling to interact with external systems
expected_output: LLM agents use tool calling (function calling) to delegate specific tasks to external systems. The model generates a structured JSON call specifying a tool name and arguments; the runtime executes the tool and returns the result to the model. This enables agents to search the web, read files, query databases, and take other actions beyond token prediction.
notes: Well-defined technical topic covered in the LangChain PDF.

expected_output: RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) adds external knowledge retrieval at inference time. LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) is a parameter-efficient fine-tuning technique that injects trainable rank-decomposition matrices into a frozen model. RAG is suitable for dynamic knowledge; LoRA is suitable for adapting a model to a fixed task or style.
notes: Narrow overlap: RAG is in local knowledge base, LoRA is not — requires web search.

expected_output: Мультиагентні системи — це архітектури, де кілька спеціалізованих агентів на основі ШІ взаємодіють для вирішення складних завдань. Кожен агент виконує окрему роль (планування, дослідження, критика), а їх координація дозволяє досягати результатів, недоступних одному агенту.
notes: Ukrainian-language query. Tests language handling — the plan may produce English queries.

input: Give me a one-sentence summary of everything in your knowledge base
expected_output: The knowledge base covers retrieval-augmented generation, large language models, and LangChain, focusing on RAG architectures, retrieval strategies, and LLM-based agent systems.
notes: Extremely broad query — tests graceful handling of open-ended requests.

input: Is GPT-5 better than Claude 3 for RAG tasks?
expected_output: This comparison depends on benchmarks, use case, and retrieval pipeline design. As of mid-2025, both GPT-5 and Claude 3 Opus perform well on RAG benchmarks; GPT-5 tends to excel on tool use while Claude 3 shows stronger long-context faithfulness. Specific results vary by dataset and configuration.
notes: Requires fresh web data. Local knowledge base has no information about GPT-5 or Claude 3 head-to-head comparisons.

input: Explain RAG to me as if I were a 5-year-old
expected_output: RAG is like giving an AI a library card. Instead of trying to remember everything, the AI looks up answers in books before speaking, so it can give you more accurate and up-to-date information.
notes: Simplified explanation request. Tests whether the agent adapts tone/style appropriately.

input: Write a poem about my cat
notes: Completely off-domain creative request. The agent should either decline or produce an irrelevant answer with low relevancy score.
